DeepSeek-Coder-33B). ❷ Generalizability. The capability learned
by CoLT can generalize to new languages (i.e., languages not in
training data). Besides, CoLT is model-agnostic and effectively im-
proves multiple LLMs. ❸ Enhanced Context Utilization Capability.
CoLT significantly improves the capability of LLMs in utilizing

Background. Repository-level (Repo-level) code completion aims
to complete an unfinished code file based on the code contexts of
the repository [12, 26, 39]. The input of repo-level code completion
typically is a long code context containing thousands of tokens. In
recent years, Large Language Models have achieved State-Of-The-
Art (SOTA) performance in code completion, e.g., aiXcoder-7B [21].
To process such long code contexts, the context windows of LLMs
are often large, e.g., 16,384 tokens.
Motivations. However, we found that existing LLMs may ignore
the information within long contexts in repo-level code completion.
Our motivation stems from two observations. First, recent studies
[25, 36] of the natural language process have revealed that LLMs
struggle to utilize all the information provided in long contexts
effectively. For example, LLMs fail to pass simple probing tasks such
as Needle-in-the-Haystack, which asks LLMs to recall specific texts
in long contexts. Second, we conducted a preliminary experiment
and found similar problems in repo-level code completion. For
example, LLMs overlook the relevant APIs and similar code in
input contexts. Section 2 shows two examples and detailed analyses.
However, recent studies [26, 34, 39] focus on how to extract relevant
contexts and ignore this inherent limitation of LLMs.
We think the above limitation is caused by the unintentional bias
hidden in the pre-training of LLMs. Current pre-training pipelines
concatenate multiple code files to make a training sequence and
train LLMs to generate the sequence auto-regressively. However,
the correlation between these code files within a training sequence
is often sparse or nonexistent. For example, StarCoder [23] ran-
domly samples files from 300 million files to construct a training
sequence. It means the prior files provide little information for
predicting the subsequent files. Such pre-training data may inadver-
tently introduce a bias into LLMs, i.e., relying on nearby contexts

invoke the relevant API in the input context, leading to sub-
optimal results.
2 Motivating Example
As stated in Section 1, our motivation is that existing LLMs may
ignore the relevant information within long contexts in repo-level
code completion. In this section, we conduct a case study to show
our motivation.
Motivating Example ❶: LLMs fail to invoke the relevant
API in input contexts, leading to suboptimal results. Figure
1 shows a real-world example of repo-level code completion. As

the human-written ground truth. Human developers refer to the
similar function and write the correct path to the user’s data storage.
However, both LLMs ignore the valuable information in the similar
function and output wrong completions.
Through the above two examples, we think that existing LLMs
struggle to fully utilize the long context in repo-level code comple-
tion. Recent studies [25, 36] of natural language processing also
found similar phenomena and validated our motivation.
As stated in Section 1, we attribute the above problem to the
unintentional bias hidden in the pre-training of LLMs, i.e., relying
on nearby contexts and ignoring long-range contexts. To address
the inherent bias in LLMs, we propose a novel fine-tuning approach
named CoLT. CoLT leverages reinforcement learning to explicitly
train LLMs to utilize the long context. We apply CoLT to a SOTA
LLM - aiXcoder-7B and present aiXcoder-7B-v2. aiXcoder-7B-v2
achieves new SOTA results in repo-level code completion. Figure
1 (b) and 2 (b) show the outputs of aiXcoder-7B-v2. Compared to
aiXcoder-7B, aiXcoder-7B-v2 successfully utilizes the information
in long contexts and outputs correct results.
3  CoLT
In this section, we propose a novel fine-tuning approach for repo-
level code completion, named CoLT. CoLT trains LLMs to fully uti-
lize the information provided in the long contexts, thus improving
the performance of LLMs. Because CoLT is a data-driven solution,
we also collect and release a new repo-level code completion dataset
named CoLT-132K.
3.1  CoLT-132K
We first present the proposed dataset and its collection process.
3.1.1  Data Statistics. CoLT-132K is a new repo-level code com-
pletion dataset. Each sample in CoLT-132K consists of the long code
contexts as inputs and the ground truth code. Models are asked to

e Completion  Conference’17, July 2017, Washington, DC, USA
predict the ground truth code based on long contexts. Compared to
previous repo-level code completion datasets, CoLT-132K features
three key characteristics:
• Large-Scale. Constructed from 2,000 high-quality open-source
repositories, CoLT-132K contains 120,000 training and 12,000
testing samples, establishing the largest corpus for repo-level
code completion to date.
• Long Contexts. Each sample has long code contexts as input,
comprising in-file context and cross-file context. The average
length of input contexts is approximately 12,000 tokens, and the
maximum length is over 128,000 tokens. For comparison, a pop-
ular repo-level code completion benchmark - CrossCodeEval’s
input contexts average 3390 tokens [11]. CoLT provides a more
challenging code completion scenario for LLMs.
• High Diversity. The diversity of CoLT-132K is shown in two
aspects. On the one hand, CoLT-132K covers four popular pro-
gramming languages, including Python, Java, C++, and Go.

On
the other hand, under each language, CoLT-132K contains three
completion scenarios: (i) API invocation: completing a line of code
containing cross-file API invocations; (ii) Single line: completing a
randomly selected line of code; (iii) Structured span: completing a
structured span [21] containing multiple lines of code. Each com-
pletion scenario under a language has 10,000 training and 1,000
testing samples. Figure 4 shows examples of three completion
scenarios. We believe that high diversity will make CoLT-132K
cover more real-world code completion scenarios for training
and testing.
To avoid data leakage, the training and test samples are collected
from different repositories. The test samples are from repositories
1
created after March 2024 .
3.1.2  Data Collection Pipeline. As shown in Figure 3, the data
collection pipeline of CoLT-132K consists of four stages:
Stage 1: Code Repository Crawling. In this stage, we crawl
code repositories from GitHub. We design the following rules to
ensure the quality and diversity of repositories:
• Multilingual Data. Python, Java, C++, and Go are four popular
programming languages in modern software development [14].
Thus, we prioritize repositories in these languages to establish a
representative corpus.
• License Compliance. Repositories must adopt permissive open-
source licenses (e.g., MIT) to avoid legal issues. Repositories with
restrictive licenses (e.g., GPL) or those with unclear licensing
terms are excluded. We use GHArchive [2] to cross-check the
license type and ensure compliance with permissive standards.
• Popularity. Repositories are selected based on their popularity.
We retain repositories that have at least 10 stars, and updates
within the last two years. Then we score repositories based on
the number of stars, commit frequency, and the presence of test
files. Repositories are sorted by their scores, and the lowest 10%
are removed as they are considered low-quality.
Stage 2: Repo-level Deduplication. Duplicate data will affect
model training, leading to overfitting [29]. Therefore, we conduct
the repo-level data deduplication, which consists of two steps:

• Exact deduplication. We remove repositories with identical
contents. Specifically, we discard repositories where all files have
the same content.
• Near deduplication. Exact deduplication may cause false nega-
tives, so we further perform near deduplication. We compute the
MinHash [8] of all files with 256 permutations and use Locality
Sensitive Hashing (LSH) [19] to identify clusters of duplicates
across repositories. We further reduce these clusters by ensuring
that each file in the original cluster is similar to at least one other
file in the reduced cluster. We consider two files near-duplicate
when their Jaccard similarity [20] exceeds 0.85.
Stage 3: Dependency Graph Construction. We use SCIP [4]
to parse these repositories. Repositories that fail to parse or ex-
ceed the time limit are excluded. The motivations of this stage are
two-fold. First, we exclude incomplete repositories. Second, the
structured output of SCIP contains the locations of various code
elements within the repository (e.g., classes and functions) and the
dependencies between these elements (e.g., function invocations).
This information can then support the subsequent stages.
the output of SCIP is a structured JSON file that
Stage 4: Ground Truth Code Extraction. In this stage, we
extract the ground truth code to be completed. To cover diverse
completion scenarios in the real world, we select the following
three types of code as ground truth:
• API Invocation. Based on the dependency graphs built in stage
3, we identify code lines that contain cross-file API invocations.
These lines are then extracted as the ground truth code.
• Single Line. We randomly select a single line of code as the
ground truth code, ensuring it meets the following criteria: it

Li et al.
oLT-132K Data Collection.
must not be empty, a comment, or an import statement; its length
must be between 5 and 100 tokens.

This selection process avoids
both too-easy lines (e.g., empty or very short) and too-hard lines
(e.g., comments, imports, or very long lines).
• Structured Spans. Following previous work [21], we first parse
the code into an AST. Then, we randomly select a non-root and
non-leaf AST node (e.g., an if-statement block or method body)
and extract its corresponding code span. Additionally, the span
is filtered to exclude empty blocks, comments, and import state-
ments and constrained to 5–100 tokens in length.
We also take the following measures to improve the diversity of
CoLT-132K:
• To mitigate potential data imbalance, where many samples origi-
nate from a single, large repository, we implement uniform sam-
pling across all repositories.
• We perform near deduplication on the ground truth code using
token-level Jaccard similarity, removing entries with a similarity
score above 0.85.
Stage 5: Long Contexts Extraction. The goal of this stage is to
construct input contexts corresponding to the ground truth code. As
stated in Section 3.1.1, the input contexts comprise in-file contexts
and cross-file contexts. We leverage the following strategies to
extract these two types of contexts:
• In-file Contexts. Following previous work [21], we locate the
current file (i.e., the file containing the ground truth code) and
extract its prefix and suffix as in-file contexts. The prefix refers
to the code preceding the ground truth code, while the suffix is
the code following the ground truth code.
• Cross-file Context: Extracting cross-file contexts is an open
problem in repo-level code completion. Following previous works
[12, 26, 39], we adopt two mainstream strategies for extracting
cross-file context:
(1) Dependency-based Strategy. The motivation is that im-
ported files often contain elements used by the current file,
such as functions. Thus, based on the dependency graphs
built in Stage 3, we extract external files imported by the
current file as cross-file contexts.
(2) Retrieval-based Strategy. The core idea of this strategy
is to retrieve similar code snippets across the repository as
cross-file contexts. Following previous works [26, 39], we
divide the entire repository into multiple code snippets by
splitting it into fixed-size windows of 20 lines. These code
snippets form a retrieval corpus. From the end of the in-file
prefix, we extract a code snippet that serves as the query.
We then calculate the Jaccard similarity between the query
and each snippet in the retrieval corpus at the token level,
ranking the snippets based on similarity. The top 10 most
similar code snippets are selected as cross-file contexts.

3.2  Reinforcement Learning Training
In this section, we describe the training process of CoLT. We first
present the training data construction (Section 3.2.1). Then, we
show the two-stage training of CoLT in Section 3.2.
3.2.1  Data Pre-Processing. We first describe the data pre-processing
for converting input contexts into prompts.
Prompt Construction. It aims to combine the in-file context
and cross-file context into a complete prompt. Previous works
[26, 39] convert cross-file contexts into comments and concate-
nate cross-file contexts with in-file contexts, obtaining prompts.
However, this approach has two limitations: (i) converting cross-
file contexts into comments may hinder LLMs from utilizing the
contexts, since the commented code in pre-training data is often
irrelevant. (ii) Cross-file contexts typically are very long and often
exceed the context window of LLMs.
To address the above limitations, we propose an approach to con-
structing prompts. Compared to previous works, our approach has
two differences.

First, we format cross-file contexts as independent
files, rather than converting them into comments. This approach
better aligns with the context format seen during pre-training. Ad-
ditionally, we perform moderate compression of cross-file contexts.

e Completion  Conference’17, July 2017, Washington, DC, USA
As mentioned in Section 3.1, we extract imported files as cross-file
contexts, which may contain relevant functions. For functions in
imported files, we only keep their signatures and comments and
remove their bodies. Our motivation is that LLMs can know how to
invoke a function from its signature and comments, without having
to understand implementation details.
Applying the above data processing to CoLT-132K, we obtain
the training data. Each training sample consists of a prompt and
the ground-truth code.
3.2.2  Two-Stage Training Process. CoLT employs a two-stage
training process, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) as a warm-
up stage and Reinforcement Learning (RL) to further enhance the
model’s capability in utilizing long contexts. Thus, we first split
the training data into two parts in a 5:5 ratio for two stages. An
overview of the training process is shown in Algorithm 1.
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT, Line 1-6 inAlgorithm1). This
stage aims to warm up models by fine-tuning models on repo-level
code completion datasets. Existing works [15, 37] have shown that
the warm-up is crucial for stabilizing the subsequent reinforcement
learning training. Through this stage, we expect that models can
know the repo-level code completion task and generate plausible
completions. Specifically, we fine-tune models by minimizing the
following loss function:

where I𝑖 and O𝑖 denote the -th prompt and ground truth code in
training data. 𝑁 is the size of training data.
Reinforcement Learning (RL, Line 7-21 in Algorithm 1).
This stage aims to leverage RL to further improve the model’s
capability in utilizing long contexts. We employ a popular offline
RL approach - Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [30]. DPO can
refine the model’s behavior without an explicit reward model and
has shown impressive results in many tasks such as mathematical
reasoning [22, 35]. Specifically, DPO collects lots of preference
data, which comprises triples - { prompt, chosen code, rejected
code }. The chosen code is the ground truth code, and the rejected
code is incorrect code caused by ignoring long contexts. DPO will
increase the likelihood of generating the chosen code and decrease
the likelihood of generating the rejected code.
To support RL training, we first collect the preference data. Each
∗  −  ∗
sample is a triple {I, O , O }. I and O are input prompts and chosen
−
code (i.e., the ground truth code) in training data. O denotes the
rejected code. To obtain the rejected code, we use the SFT-tuned
model to generate multiple candidate completions for each prompt.
We select high-quality rejected code from these candidate comple-
tions through rigorous filtration. The filtration excludes three types
of completions: (i) empty completions, (ii) same completions as
ground truth code, and (iii) completions containing ground truth
code.
Based on the preference data, we optimize the model by mini-
mizing the following loss function:

To evaluate the effectiveness of CoLT, we conduct a large-scale
study. In this section, we describe the design of our study, including
research questions, benchmarks, baselines, and evaluation metrics.
5.1  Research Questions
Our study aims to answer the following research questions (RQs):
RQ1: How much improvement does CoLT achieve in repo-
level code completion? We aim to evaluate the improvement in
repo-level code completion performance when using CoLT. We
apply CoLT to aiXcoder-7B and present aiXcoder-CoLT. Then we
compare it to existing SOTA LLMs on multiple benchmarks.
RQ2: How effectively does CoLT generalize across different
programming languages? This RQ investigates whether the con-
text utilization capabilities learned by CoLT can generalize across
languages. We train aiXcoder-7B-v2 on CoLT-132K covering four
languages and test the model on other languages.
RQ3: Does CoLT maintain its effectiveness across differ-
ent LLMs? Besides the aiXcoder-7B, we apply CoLT to other two
popular LLMs (i.e., DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B and Code Llama-7B) to
evaluate whether CoLT still can improve them in repo-level code
completion.
RQ4: Does CoLT improve the capability of LLMs to utilize
long contexts? This RQ aims to evaluate whether CoLT enhances
the LLMs’ capability in utilizing long contexts. We design two
experiments to investigate whether aiXcoder-7B-v2 can effectively
utilize the APIs and similar code in long contexts.
5.2  Benchmarks
We conduct experiments on the following benchmarks:
CoLT-132K (Test) is the test set of CoLT-132K collected by this
paper. It covers four programming languages (i.e., Python, Java,
C/C++, Go) and three completion scenarios (i.e., API invocation,
single line, and spans), with a total of 12,000 samples. Each com-
pletion scenario under a language contains 1,000 samples. Notably,
the test set was meticulously collected from repositories created
after May 2024 to prevent data leakage. For detailed information,
please refer to Section 3.1.

6.1  RQ1: Improvements in Repo-level Code
Completion
Setting. As stated in Section 4, we apply CoLT to aiXcoder-7B
and present aiXcoder-7B-v2. To validate the effectiveness of CoLT,
we compare aiXcoder-7B-v2 to seven LLMs on repo-level code
completion.
Results and Analyses. The results are shown in Table 1, and 2.
We also show the relative improvements of aiXcoder-7B-v2 com-
pared to the strongest 7B model.
CoLT substantially improves the performance of aiXcoder-
7B on repo-level code completion. aiXcoder-7B-v2 substantially
outperforms aiXcoder-7B on different languages and completion
scenarios. Especially when completing Python’s structured spans,
aiXcoder-7B-v2 outperforms aiXcoder-7B by 44% in EM and 23.6%
in BLEU. The obvious improvements show that CoLT can effectively
improve the performance of LLMs in repo-level code completion.
aiXcoder-7B-v2 achieves new SOTA results on repo-level
code completion, surpassing even larger models. aiXcoder-7B-
v2 outperforms existing 7Bmodels in four languages and three com-
pletion scenarios. In structured spans, it outperforms the strongest
7B model by up to 34.4% in EM and 23.6% in BLEU within C++.

on repo-level code completion. Specifically, aiXcoder-7B-v2
outperforms aiXcoder-7B by up to 44% in EM and 23.6% in

on repo-level code completion, surpassing even larger models
(e.g., DeepSeek-Coder-32B).

The context utilization ability learned by CoLT can gener-
alize to new languages. Even in languages not included in the

strates strong performance.

6.3  RQ3: Effectiveness Across Different LLMs
Setting. We apply CoLT to Deepseek-Coder-6.7B and Code Llama-
7B. The training details are consistent with aiXcoder-7B-v2 (Sec-
tion 4). Then, we compare the performance of these models with
and without CoLT on the CoLT-132K test set.
Results and Analyses. Table 4 shows the results.
CoLT brings substantial improvements on both LLMs. The
application of CoLT to both LLMs brings significant improvements
in both EM and BLEU across all completion scenarios. For instance,
when DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B predicts Python’s structured spans, its
EM is improved by 70.8% and BLEU by 32.6%. Similar trends are
observed in Java (+146.9% EM, +72.7% BLEU) and C++ (+242.6%
EM, +108.5% BLEU) in Code Llama-7B.

These results show the
effectiveness of CoLT across different LLMs.
Answer to RQ3: CoLT effectively improves different LLMs.
For example, applying CoLT to Deepseek-Coder-6.7B yields
substantial performance improvements, with EM improve-
ments of up to 123.6% and BLEU gains of 67.4%.
6.4  RQ4: Improvements of Long Context
Utilization
This RQ aims to conduct an in-depth analysis to demonstrate that
CoLT enhances themodel’s ability to utilize long contexts. As stated
in Section 2, we found that existing LLMs struggle to utilize the
relevant APIs and similar code in the contexts. Thus, we design
two experiments to evaluate the performance of aiXcoder-7B and
aiXcoder-7B-v2 in utilizing these contexts.
❶ The performance in utilizing APIs in contexts.

Setting. In the CoLT-132K, the APIs to be completed by LLMs are
all defined in the input contexts, requiring the use of long context.
To quantify the model’s ability to complete these APIs, we introduce
a new metric: API Accuracy (API Acc). It measures the accuracy
of API names in the completion results. If the predicted API name
matches the ground truth, it is counted as 1; otherwise, it remains
0. This metric focuses specifically on API accuracy, excluding other
factors (e.g., arguments).
Results and Analyses. As shown in Table 5, across all four pro-
gramming languages, aiXcoder-7B-v2 achieves the highest API Acc,
outperforming other models by a substantial margin. Specifically,
aiXcoder-7B-v2 surpasses aiXcoder-7B by 13.2% on Python, 6.8%
on Java, 14.5% on C++, and 6.4% on Go. These results highlight the
effectiveness of CoLT in improving the model’s ability to invoke
relevant APIs in context.
❷ The performance in utilizing similar code.
Setting. In this experiment, we explore whether CoLT enhances
the model’s ability to utilize similar code snippets in long contexts.
We categorize all test samples into ten groups based on the Code-
BLEU scores between the similar code and the ground truth
code. Higher similarity means the similar code contains more use-
ful information for the model. For each group, we count the number
of correct completions by aiXcoder-7B and aiXcoder-7B-v2.
Results and Analyses. The results are presented in Figure 6.
aiXcoder-7B-v2 outperforms aiXcoder-7B across all groups. For
example, when completing structured spans, aiXcoder-7B-v2 out-
performs aiXcoder-7B by up to 99 correct completions. We also
notice that the improvements of CoLT in structured spans are more
obvious than ones in other scenarios. This is possibly due to the
inherent challenge of predicting structured spans. It is hard to com-
plete structured spans without utilizing the information in input
contexts. After introducing CoLT, aiXcoder-7B-v2 learns how to
utilize contexts and thus shows obvious improvements in predicting
structured spans.
Answer to RQ4: CoLT significantly improves the context
utilization capability of aiXcoder-7B. For example, aiXcoder-
7B-v2 shows better performance in invoking relevant APIs
and utilizing similar code in long contexts..

7.1  Learned Lessons for Practitioners
We encountered several failures throughout the fine-tuning. Based
on these experiences, we summarize the following practical lessons
and hope these lessons can help practitioners train LLMs.
❶ Data diversity is crucial for the generalizability of mod-
els. Our preliminary experiments show that insufficient data di-
versity may lead to model overfitting. Based on our experiments,
we recommend practitioners prioritize the following aspects to
improve data diversity: (i) Domain diversity. The data should come
from repositories in various domains. Meanwhile, an upper limit
should be set for each repository to avoid a large number of samples
coming from a large repository. (ii) Language diversity. The data
should cover as many programming languages as possible such as
front-end languages and back-end languages.
❷Reinforcement learning is sensitive tohyper-parameters.
Our experiments show that reinforcement learning in code comple-
tion is highly sensitive to hyperparameters. Based on our empirical
results, we summarize the recommended settings of the following
hyperparameters:
• Learning rate. A lower learning rate typically is better. Our ex-
−6
periments show that a lower learning rate (e.g., 2 × 10  ) leads
to more stable training and superior performance on validation
−5
data compared to a larger learning rate (e.g., 5 × 10  ).
• Training epoch. For the RL training in code completion, a smaller
epoch is better. In our experiments, we observe a clear decline in
validation loss as the number of epochs increases. More epochs
are prone to model overfitting.
• 𝛽 in RL loss (Equation 2). The 𝛽 controls how aggressively the
model updates its behaviour during RL training. In our experi-
ments, a higher 𝛽 (e.g., 0.9) is better than a lower 𝛽 (e.g., 0.1) in
code completion. One possible reason is that a lower 𝛽 allows
more aggressive parameter updates, which harms the capability
of models in code completion. By using a higher 𝛽, the model
can retain its basic capability in code completion while learning
to utilize the long contexts.

7.2  Threats to Validity
There are three potential threats to the validity of our work:
The settings of hyper-parameter. As stated in Section 7.1,
hyper-parameters significantly impact the stability of training. Con-
sidering that the training cost is not trivial, exhaustively searching
for optimal hyper-parameters is impractical. Therefore, we em-

The current settings may not be optimal, and further tuning could
potentially enhance CoLT’s performance.
Potential data leakage. A key threat in evaluating code comple-
tion models is data leakage between test sets in our dataset and the
LLMs training data. To address this, CoLT-132K’s test set comes ex-
clusively from repositories created after March 2024, which is later
than the training data cutoff of all models used in our experiments.
Therefore, we believe this approach effectivelyminimizes the risk of
data leakage and ensures the reliability of our experimental results.
8  Related Work
Repository-level code completion has emerged as a critical chal-
lenge in software engineering, aiming to enhance the accuracy of
code completion by leveraging the broader context of a repository.
Prior studies have predominantly focused on the retrieval of rele-
vant context from the repository, with various strategies proposed
to identify and extract pertinent code snippets [6, 9, 24, 34]. For
instance, RepoCoder [39] introduces an iterative retrieval and gen-
eration approach, where an initial completion is generated and
subsequently used to retrieve relevant context. It is then prepended
as a comment to the prompt. Similarly, CrossCodeEval [11] pro-
poses a straightforward retrieval method, using the lines preceding
the cursor as a query to extract and append similar code snippets.
GraphCoder [26] advances this by constructing a Code Context
Graph (CCG) to represent the repository, enabling more sophisti-
cated retrieval based on control flow, data flow, and dependencies.
While these approaches have made significant strides in context
retrieval, how to effectively utilize the retrieved context remains
underexplored. Most existing studies view LLMs as a “perfect” gen-
erator and simply input long contexts into LLMs. However, this
simplistic approach may not fully exploit the potential of the re-
trieved information. For example, FT2Ra [16] proposes a novel

In this paper, we reveal a severe limitation of LLMs in repo-level
code completion, i.e., LLMs struggle to utilize the relevant informa-
tion (e.g., APIs or similar code) within long contexts. To address this
limitation, we propose a fine-tuning approach named CoLT. CoLT
is a reinforcement learning-based solution that explicitly encour-
ages models to utilize the information within long contexts and
punishes models for ignoring long contexts. To support CoLT, we
release a repo-level code completion dataset - CoLT-132K consisting
of 132,000 samples. Extensive experiments show that CoLT substan-
tially improves the performance of existing LLMs (e.g., aiXcoder-7B)
on repo-level code completion.
In the future, we will further improve the ability of LLMs to
process lengthy code contexts. On the one hand, we plan to explore
effective approaches to expanding the context window of LLMs.
On the other hand, we will study new training techniques to help
LLMs learn long-range dependencies.
